Were You Asked This?
Design a Logger System

Design a logger system that receives messages with timestamps and only prints each message if it hasn't been printed in the last 10 seconds, with support for multiple applications and search capabilities.

Asked at:

Apple

Google

Microsoft

Amazon

A centralized logging service is a platform that collects, deduplicates, stores, and lets you search application logs in real time—think Splunk, Datadog Logs, or CloudWatch Logs. In this variant, the twist is a 10‑second suppression window: only print a message if it hasn’t been printed in the last 10 seconds, while supporting multiple applications (multi‑tenant) and fast search.

Interviewers ask this to test whether you can turn a seemingly simple requirement into a robust, scalable, and cost‑aware design. You’ll be evaluated on stream ingestion at high write rates, time‑window deduplication (like a rate limiter), multi‑tenant isolation, search indexing, and handling concurrency and ordering across instances and regions. Expect to reason about correctness vs. consistency, backpressure, hot keys, and retention—without leaning on vendor magic.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to send log messages with timestamps and receive an immediate allow/deny decision based on whether an identical message was printed in the last 10 seconds.

Users should be able to scope logs by application (multi-tenant), ensuring isolation of quotas, search, and access controls across apps or teams.

Users should be able to search and filter logs by time range, application, severity, and full-text terms, with relevant results returned quickly.

Users should be able to live-tail recent logs for a given application or filter to observe near real-time activity.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you implement the 10-second suppression window correctly at scale across many stateless log processors and regions?

How will you handle high write throughput and backpressure without losing logs or blowing up latency?

How will you support fast, cost-effective search over large volumes of logs while keeping storage under control?

How do you prevent noisy-neighbor issues and hot-key contention across multiple applications?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Writes

Logs are a high-ingest, append-heavy workload with bursty traffic. You must decouple producers from consumers, partition the stream, and use batching and backpressure to keep latency predictable while avoiding data loss.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

The 10-second dedup creates hot keys when many instances process the same message. You need sharding, atomic operations, and key design to avoid thundering herds and race conditions.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Search across large time ranges and tenants requires sharding, time-based indices, and query pruning to keep search latency low and costs under control.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis provides sub-millisecond, atomic operations (SETNX with EX, Lua scripts) ideal for the 10-second suppression window and per-message keys. TTL-based expiry simplifies cleanup and supports high-QPS access with predictable latency.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka is a durable, horizontally scalable log that absorbs bursts, preserves ordering per key, and enables replay. It decouples ingestion from processing and indexing, which is critical for reliability and backpressure control in logging pipelines.

Read More
Elasticsearch

Elasticsearch is built for log search with inverted indices, time-based sharding, and schema control. It supports low-latency queries over large datasets with filters on time ranges and fields like application and severity.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Rate Limiter

The 10-second suppression is a rate-limiting window per key. Techniques like token buckets, Redis SETNX/TTL, and consistent hashing directly map to this logger’s dedup logic.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Both systems handle high-throughput, near real-time streams, where ordering, fan-out, and live-tail/display with low latency are essential under heavy contention.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

Click pipelines require deduplication, windowed processing, and cost-effective storage with search/analytics—very similar ingestion and indexing challenges as log systems.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Relying on in-process memory for the 10-second window

This breaks as soon as you scale horizontally—different instances will print duplicates. It also loses state on restarts. Use a shared, atomic store and partitioning to ensure consistent decisions.

Tying ingestion success to synchronous search indexing

If the request path blocks on Elasticsearch, any indexing hiccup or shard pressure will cause elevated latency and dropped logs. Decouple with a durable queue and asynchronous indexing with retries and idempotency.

Vague message identity and timestamp semantics

If you don’t define the dedup key (e.g., appId + normalized message text) and whether to use arrival time vs. client time, you’ll see incorrect suppression under clock skew and formatting differences. Specify the key and time source up front.

Mid-level

Design a logger system that receives a stream of messages with timestamps. Each message should be printed if and only if it has not been printed in the last 10 seconds. Given a message and a timestamp (in seconds granularity), return true if the message should be printed, otherwise return false. Handle messages arriving roughly at the same time.
